unit.name: Parametric surfaces and the explicit–implicit trade-off
unit.description: Explains parametric curves and surfaces, then contrasts the strengths of explicit versus implicit representations.
unit.summary: Wu next introduces parametric representations, where a lower-dimensional parameter space is mapped into 3D; the key idea is that many shapes have lower intrinsic dimensionality than the ambient space [ts=698s][ts=731s]. He uses a circle and a sphere as examples, then mentions Bézier curves and Bézier surfaces as flexible smooth representations controlled by a small number of control points [ts=760s][ts=783s][ts=798s]. This leads to a broader comparison: explicit representations directly produce points on the surface, which makes sampling easy because one can plug parameters into the function [ts=863s][ts=941s]. However, explicit forms make inside/outside queries difficult, whereas implicit forms define the surface by a condition such as f(x,y,z)=0, making membership-style queries easier and foreshadowing why implicit functions are attractive for later neural rendering methods [ts=981s][ts=1064s][ts=1172s].

key_points.text: Parametric representations map lower-dimensional intrinsic variables into 3D shape geometry.
A circle can be represented parametrically by varying a single variable rather than storing samples.
A sphere in 3D needs two degrees of freedom, typically u and v.
Bézier curves and surfaces use a small number of control points to model smooth geometry.
Explicit representations make sampling surface points easy.
Explicit representations make inside/outside tests hard, motivating implicit geometry.
Implicit geometry represents a surface by a constraint such as f(x,y,z)=0.

kp_catalog.name: Parametric curves and surfaces
kp_catalog.description: Representations that map a low-dimensional parameter domain such as t or (u,v) into points on a 2D or 3D shape.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because parametric curves and surfaces clarifies representations that map a low-dimensional parameter domain such as t or (u,v) into points on a 2D or 3D shape, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Implicit functions for geometry
kp_catalog.description: A representation in which surface points satisfy a condition like f(x,y,z)=0, enabling inside/outside reasoning and later neural field methods.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because implicit functions for geometry formalizes how a representation in which surface points satisfy a condition like f(x,y,z)=0, enabling inside/outside reasoning and later neural field methods, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Explicit vs. implicit geometry taxonomy
kp_catalog.description: The organizing distinction between representations that directly enumerate or parametrize surface points and representations that define geometry through a function or field.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because explicit vs. implicit geometry taxonomy formalizes how the organizing distinction between representations that directly enumerate or parametrize surface points and representations that define geometry through a function or field, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
